---
title: "The Perceptron"
date: "2026-03-14"
author: "Julian M. Del Fiore"
categories: [classification]
---

The perceptron is a classification algorithm, so given an input $x$, we want to produce a label $\hat{y}$.
In particular, it is a supervised model, meaning that we will have a labelled training set, where we know the true value $y$ for each sample.

In general, we can choose how to represent $y$.
For binary classification problems, some typical choices include $\{0, 1\}$ and $\{-1, 1\}$.
Using one or the other does not affect the generality of the conclusions.
We will alternate between both, selecting the representation that leads to the easiest interpretation in each section.

## Predicting

We first need to understand how, given an input $x$, the model predicts a label $\hat{y}$.
In short, this happens:

1. using a linear transformation
2. selecting an activation function
3. determinining a classification criteria
4. identifying the decision regions based on the criteria

Next we dive into each of these steps.

### Linear transformation

As we get a sample as input, the value of the features $x$ represent the "concrete knowledge" we have about this example.

We want to combine the information we have about $x$ in a meaningful way.
Ultimately, we hope that this will allow us to classify $x$ correctly.

To synthesise all the knowledge we have about $x$ into a unique measure, similar to what is done in many other models, the perceptron uses a linear transformation

$$z = wx + b$$

where

 * $x$ is the input, and;
 * $w$ and $b$ are the weight and bias of the model, respectively.

The union of $w$ and $b$ are the parameters of the model.

Relying on this linear transformation, the input space $x$ gets projected onto a one-dimensional space defined by $z$.

### Activation function

<!-- The activation function determines how the model’s internal score is turned into an output — whether as a probability, a decision, or a non-linear response. -->

With the linear transformation, we condense the knowledge of $x$ into $z$.
But as this happens, we now need a way of understanding what $z$ is trying to tell us.

To complete that piece of the puzzle, the $z$-value is sent through an activation function $\activation(z)$.

In the case of the perceptron, the $\sgn(z)$ or _step_ function show in @fig-sign is used

$$
\activation(z) = \sgn(z) =
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } z \ge 0 \\
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$ {#eq-sign}

![Activation function $\sgn(z)$.](/assets/classification/perceptron/figures/sign.png){#fig-sign width=50%}

### Classification criteria

Finally, we need to tell whether we map $x$ to either $\hat{y} = 0$ or $\hat{y} = 1$.

In the perceptron, the activation function itself produces discrete outputs, so it directly defines the classification rule

$$\hat{y} = \sgn(z) = \activation(z) =
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } z \ge 0 \\
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

We typically place a decision threshold in some value $z = z_0$.
This threshold divides the real line into two regions, each corresponding to a predicted class.

In the perceptron, the decision threshold lies in $z_0 = 0$.
This can be seen in @fig-decision-region.

![The decision threshold lies at $z = 0$ (the shaded regions in 2D are just to reinforce the idea).](/assets/classification/perceptron/figures/decision_region_zspace.png){#fig-decision-region width=65%}

### Decision regions and boundary

The decision threshold in the transformed $z$-space corresponds to a geometric boundary in the input $x$-space.
In particular, the boundary lies in

$$z_0 = 0 \Leftrightarrow w x_0 + b = 0 \Leftrightarrow$$

and this also determines the decision regions

$$\hat{y} =
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } w x_0 + b \ge 0 \\
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

Focusing on $x \in \mathbb{R}$ for now, we can find $x_0$ directly as $x_0 = -\frac{b}{w}$.
The animation in @fig-decision-boundary-1d allows to see how the position of $x_0$ changes depending on the values assigned to $w$ and $b$.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
activation = z => z >= 0 ? 1 : 0;
```
::: {#fig-decision-boundary-1d}
{{< include ../_shared/decision-plot_1d.qmd >}}
Decision boundary — adjust $w$ and $b$ to explore different configurations.
:::

## Training

Recall that this is a supervised algorithm.
This means that during training, we need to have labeled samples.
Assuming that's the case, for any training example $x$, we know the true label $y$.

For each example we run through our classifier, we get a prediction $\hat{y}$.
Since we get the true labels, we are able to compare $\hat{y}$ against the expected $y$.
The more times $\hat{y} = y$, the better our model is performing.

The training is the phase in which we refine the predictions $\hat{y}$ that our classifier makes.
For that to be possible, we need to update the parameters $w$ and $b$
The hope is that, iterating, we will learn good values for $w$ and $b$, so that our predictions $\hat{y}$ are mostly correct.


To refine the values of $w$ and $b$, we need to define a loss function.

### Loss function

To know how to optimise the values of $w$ and $b$, we need to define a loss function $\loss$.
Without losing generality, and becauae the formulas look better like this, we will assume $\hat{y} \in \{-1, 1\}$ in this sub-section.


$$
\loss =
$$



The idea behind the perceptron is brute-forcing, a mindset of the type "if there is a solution, I will find it".

For an input $x$ of class $y$, the loss function is



And below some math formulas!

$$
\begin{aligned}
y &= ax + b \\
y' &= a
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-linear}

See @eq-linear.

$$
\begin{align}
y &= ax + b \\
y' &= a
\end{align}
$$

great!

Finally, n the $z$-space, it always holds that $z$ is a scalar, i.e., $z \in \mathbb{R}$.
On the contrary, $x$, and thus $w$, could belong to an $n$-dimensional space, i.e. $x, w \in \mathbb{R}^n$.

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="30%"}
```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof w1       = Inputs.range([-10, 10], {value: 0.5, step: 0.5, label: "w1"})
viewof w2       = Inputs.range([-10, 10], {value: 0.5, step: 0.5, label: "w2"})
viewof b_2d     = Inputs.range([-8, 8], {value: 0, step: 1, label: "b_2d"})
viewof plotMode = Inputs.radio(["2D", "3D"], {value: "2D", label: "View"})
```
:::

::: {.column width="70%"}
::: {#fig-decision-boundary-2d}
{{< include ../_shared/decision-plot_2d.qmd >}}
Sigmoid classifier — adjust $w$ and $b$ to explore the decision boundary.
:::
:::

::::

Play with @fig-decision-boundary-2d.
